text:
Centre for Epilepsy

Neurosciences Division 

Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr A. Rahman 
Consultant Neurologist  
Centre for Epilepsy  
KCH, Denmark Hill, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr A. Rahman, 

Alex Carter, DOB: 22-03-1987, Hospital No: K847392 NHS No. 1039485726  
Flat 12, 4 Brookfield Road, London, N16 0DL  

Diagnosis: 

Seizure disorder, current classification not established 

Aetiology: 

No clear structural, genetic, infectious, metabolic, or immune cause identified to date 

Other Medical Problems: 

Intermittent migraine without aura; mild seasonal allergic rhinitis 

Management Plan: 

1) Continue current regimen of Levetiracetam 750 mg twice daily; no brand change requested by patient.  
2) Arrange routine bloods (U&E, FBC, LFTs) prior to next visit; no historical abnormalities.  
3) Provide DVLA guidance leaflet; patient reports no events affecting driving since the isolated brief episode described below.  
4) Follow-up in six months with review of paper seizure calendar and headache diary.  

At today’s review, Alex Carter brought their paper seizure calendar and accompanying notes, which they maintain daily and bring to each appointment. Over the past year the logs show stable control with only a single, short-lived event in late August occurring after a night shift change and poor sleep. This was described as a fleeting spell with preserved awareness and no injury, settling within seconds. There have been no clusters or prolonged episodes, and no emergency care was required. The patient feels the current medication is well tolerated without mood disturbance or excessive fatigue.

Headaches have occurred roughly twice per month, responsive to simple analgesia and hydration. There is no photophobia, focal neurology, or red-flag features reported. Sleep hygiene has been a particular focus; they record bedtime and caffeine intake on the calendar, which has helped highlight triggers around irregular shift work.

We discussed maintaining the paper seizure calendar, as it provides a reliable record and has been helpful in correlating sleep disruption with breakthrough symptoms. I have not advised any medication changes today. The patient understands to seek urgent review should events increase in frequency, duration, or change in character, and to use their established safety plan.

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Over the past year the logs show stable control with only a single, short-lived event

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter explicitly states: 'Over the past year the logs show ... only a single, short-lived event in late August.' This provides a clear numeric frequency of 1 event over the past year. The event occurred in late August 2025 and the clinic date is 02 October 2025, so the patient is not seizure free for ≥6 months. Therefore, the appropriate normalized frequency is 1 per year.
seizure_frequency_number: Over the past year the logs show stable control with only a single, short-lived event

flaws.description: Gold (1 per year) disagrees with Reference (unknown)
